scryptogram.solution: a friend loveth at all times, and a brother is born for adversity.

prompt.referenceText: And Ruth said, Entreat me not to leave thee, or to return from following after thee: for whither thou goest, I will go; and where thou lodgest, I will lodge: thy people shall be my people, and thy God my God: Where thou diest, will I die, and there will I be buried: the Lord do so to me, and more also, if ought but death part thee and me.
prompt.question: Ruth had every practical reason to go home—and instead bound herself to a grieving widow with nothing left to offer her. What if some of God's richest blessings reach us not directly, but through the people we refuse to abandon? Whose 'whither thou goest' might He be asking you to say right now?
prompt.response: Ruth couldn't have known that her loyalty to one heartbroken woman would write her into the lineage of David and of Christ Himself. That's often how God blesses us—sideways, through covenant relationships we entered for love rather than gain. The hand you hold on to in someone else's worst season has a way of becoming the hand that lifts you into blessings you never saw coming.

scryptogram.solution: and let us not be weary in well doing: for in due season we shall reap, if we faint not.

prompt.referenceText: And Boaz answered and said unto her, It hath fully been shewed me, all that thou hast done unto thy mother in law since the death of thine husband: and how thou hast left thy father and thy mother, and the land of thy nativity, and art come unto a people which thou knewest not heretofore. The Lord recompense thy work, and a full reward be given thee of the Lord God of Israel, under whose wings thou art come to trust.
prompt.question: Ruth was just gathering leftover grain in a stranger's field—unglamorous, anonymous work—when God was quietly arranging a full reward. Where are you doing humble, unnoticed work and wondering if it even counts? What would change if you trusted that you've already 'come under His wings' in the ordinary?
prompt.response: Boaz saw Ruth's whole story before she asked him for anything—the quiet faithfulness no one had applauded. God blesses that kind of hidden diligence on a timetable we rarely get to see in advance. The reward isn't always immediate, but it is gathered. Sometimes the most spiritual thing you can do is keep showing up in the unremarkable field and let Him handle the recompense.

scryptogram.solution: and we know that all things work together for good to them that love god, to them who are the called according to his purpose.

prompt.referenceText: And the women said unto Naomi, Blessed be the Lord, which hath not left thee this day without a kinsman, that his name may be famous in Israel. And he shall be unto thee a restorer of thy life, and a nourisher of thine old age: for thy daughter in law, which loveth thee, which is better to thee than seven sons, hath born him.
prompt.question: Naomi had renamed herself 'Bitter' and declared she went out full and came home empty. Yet God was quietly turning that emptiness into a restored life and a name famous in Israel. What loss have you written off as final that God may still be in the middle of redeeming?
prompt.response: The book that opens with three funerals and a bitter, empty widow ends with a baby in her lap and the words 'a restorer of thy life.' God rarely erases our losses, but He is endlessly in the business of redeeming them—weaving even our emptiest seasons into something that blesses generations. Naomi's story isn't that the grief never happened; it's that the grief didn't get the last word.

scryptogram.solution: cast thy burden upon the lord, and he shall sustain thee: he shall never suffer the righteous to be moved.

prompt.referenceText: And she was in bitterness of soul, and prayed unto the Lord, and wept sore. And she vowed a vow, and said, O Lord of hosts, if thou wilt indeed look on the affliction of thine handmaid, and remember me, and not forget thine handmaid, but wilt give unto thine handmaid a man child, then I will give him unto the Lord all the days of his life.... And Hannah answered and said, No, my lord, I am a woman of a sorrowful spirit:... but have poured out my soul before the Lord.
prompt.question: Hannah didn't bring God polished words—she brought 'bitterness of soul' and wept so hard the priest assumed she was drunk. Where have you been holding back the raw version of a prayer because it felt too messy to say out loud? What might it mean that God blesses the poured-out soul, not the composed one?
prompt.response: Hannah's prayer wasn't eloquent; it was honest enough to look unhinged to a watching priest. And that was the prayer God answered. He isn't impressed by performance and isn't frightened off by anguish—'poured out my soul before the Lord' is one of scripture's truest definitions of prayer. Whatever you've been too embarrassed to say to Him, that ache is exactly the thing He has been waiting to hear.

scryptogram.solution: prove me now herewith, saith the lord of hosts, if i will not open you the windows of heaven, and pour you out a blessing, that there shall not be room enough to receive it.

prompt.referenceText: For this child I prayed; and the Lord hath given me my petition which I asked of him: Therefore also I have lent him to the Lord; as long as he liveth he shall be lent to the Lord. And he worshipped the Lord there.
prompt.question: Hannah begged for a son for years—and then handed him back to the Lord almost as soon as she had him. How can it be a blessing to give away the very thing you prayed hardest to receive? Is there a gift in your life God might be inviting you to hold more loosely, as something lent rather than owned?
prompt.response: Most of us would have clung to a long-awaited child with white knuckles. Hannah's worship was to open her hands. The paradox runs all through scripture: the blessings we consecrate back to God tend to multiply, while the ones we grip tend to shrink. Holding your gifts as 'lent to the Lord' doesn't lose them—it frees them to bless far more than they ever could inside a closed fist.

scryptogram.solution: humble yourselves in the sight of the lord, and he shall lift you up.

prompt.referenceText: The Lord maketh poor, and maketh rich: he bringeth low, and lifteth up. He raiseth up the poor out of the dust, and lifteth up the beggar from the dunghill, to set them among princes, and to make them inherit the throne of glory: for the pillars of the earth are the Lord's, and he hath set the world upon them.
prompt.question: Once-childless Hannah sings that God 'raiseth up the poor out of the dust' to 'set them among princes.' She's describing her own reversal—and a pattern. Where do you feel low, overlooked, or stuck 'in the dust' right now? What would it do to your week to really believe that lifting people up is something God delights to do?
prompt.response: Hannah's song became the very template Mary would echo centuries later about her own son. Both women learned the same thing: God specializes in reversals. The dust-to-throne arc isn't a fluke; it's His signature move. If you're in a low place, that isn't evidence He's forgotten you—in scripture, the dust is precisely where He tends to start lifting. Your current floor is not your ceiling.

scryptogram.solution: before i formed thee in the belly i knew thee; and before thou camest forth out of the womb i sanctified thee, and i ordained thee a prophet unto the nations.

prompt.referenceText: Therefore Eli said unto Samuel, Go, lie down: and it shall be, if he call thee, that thou shalt say, Speak, Lord; for thy servant heareth. So Samuel went and lay down in his place. And the Lord came, and stood, and called as at other times, Samuel, Samuel. Then Samuel answered, Speak; for thy servant heareth.
prompt.question: God called Samuel by name in the dark, three times, before the boy even knew how to answer. One of the quietest blessings in all of scripture is simply being known and called by God personally. When did you last give Him the answer Samuel finally learned—'Speak, Lord; for thy servant heareth'—instead of doing most of the talking yourself?
prompt.response: Samuel didn't recognize the voice at first; he had to be taught that the One calling him in the night was God. Many of us are the same—mistaking God's promptings for something ordinary. The blessing here is staggering if you sit with it: the Lord of hosts knows your name and speaks it. The posture that unlocks that blessing isn't louder asking; it's quieter listening. 'Speak, Lord' is a complete prayer.
